pathogen_scientific_name.quote: Bean pod mottle is caused by the comovirus Bean pod mottle virus.

type_of_disease.quote: Bean pod mottle is caused by the comovirus Bean pod mottle virus.

value: Foliar
Stem
Pod
Seed
affected_parts.quote: Symptoms of BPMV are green to yellow mottling (blotchiness) of younger leaves in the upper canopy. In severe cases leaves may show puckering and distortion and plants can be stunted. Pods can be misshapen with mottling and reduced in size. Infected plants may have green stems after the pods have matured. Seed from infected plants may appear smoky in color or have reduced size and quality due to seed coat mottling which will be brown or black depending on hilum color.

summary.value: Green to yellow mottling (blotchiness) of younger leaves in the upper canopy; in severe cases leaves show puckering and distortion and plants can be stunted; pods may be misshapen and reduced in size, and seeds may appear smoky in color or show seed coat mottling.
summary.quote: Symptoms of BPMV are green to yellow mottling (blotchiness) of younger leaves in the upper canopy. In severe cases leaves may show puckering and distortion and plants can be stunted.

diagnostic_features.value: Foliar symptoms are most obvious at cooler temperatures and often disappear when it is hot; pods can be misshapen with mottling, and infected plants may show green stems after pods have matured.
diagnostic_features.quote: Foliar symptoms are most obvious at cooler temperatures and often disappear when it is hot.

value: Consider planting insecticide-treated seed if overwintering survival of bean leaf beetles is predicted, or if bean pod mottle has been confirmed in fields previously and bean leaf beetles have been present.
Foliar-applied insecticides can manage bean leaf beetle populations and may reduce bean pod mottle.
Soybean varieties differ in BPMV tolerance, although the differences are not clearly studied.
Cultural practices such as late planting will reduce BPMV.
Insecticide seed treatments and foliar sprays targeting management of the bean leaf beetle will reduce the incidence of BPMV.
treatments.quote: Consider planting insecticide-treated seed if overwintering survival of bean leaf beetles is predicted, or if bean pod mottle has been confirmed in fields previously and bean leaf beetles have been present. Foliar-applied insecticides can manage bean leaf beetle populations and may reduce bean pod mottle.

pathogen_scientific_name.quote: Cercospora leaf blight is caused by the fungus  Cercospora kikuchii .

type_of_disease.quote: Cercospora leaf blight is caused by the fungus  Cercospora kikuchii .

value: Foliar
Stem
Pod
Seed
affected_parts.quote: The spots may coalesce to form large necrotic areas on leaves, which eventually results in defoliation of infected leaves in the upper canopy. Reddish-purple lesions may also occur on petioles, stems, and pods. Infected seeds have pink to purple discoloration on the seed coats.

summary.value: Light purple spots on upper leaf surfaces exposed to light that expand and deepen to reddish-purple or bronze, giving leaves a leathery, 'sunburned' appearance; red-brown spots may coalesce into necrotic areas causing defoliation, with reddish-purple lesions on petioles, stems, and pods, and pink to purple discoloration on seed coats.
summary.quote: The first symptom of Cercospora leaf blight is the development of light purple spots and areas on the top surface of leaves exposed to light. The discolored areas expand and deepen in color to a reddish-purple or bronze. The infected leaves appear leathery and 'sunburned'. Red-brown spots may develop on both leaf surfaces. The spots may coalesce to form large necrotic areas on leaves, which eventually results in defoliation of infected leaves in the upper canopy. Reddish-purple lesions may also occur on petioles, stems, and pods. Infected seeds have pink to purple discoloration on the seed coats.

diagnostic_features.value: Purple-bronze discoloration on the leaf surface caused by the light-dependent toxin cercosporin, with dark spots along major leaf veins and petioles; symptoms begin on sun-exposed leaves in the upper canopy and leaves appear leathery and 'sunburned'.
diagnostic_features.quote: the pathogen produces a toxin called cercosporin, which is responsible for the purple-bronze discoloration on the leaf surface ... small, dark spots will appear along major leaf veins and petioles

value: Crop rotation systems incorporating non-host plants such as corn or small grains can reduce inoculum in crop residue and disrupt the disease cycle.
Tillage is also an option to reduce inoculum levels in the field.
Soybean varieties vary in their response to C. kikuchii, but none are completely resistant to the pathogen.
Use pathogen-free seed and varieties with low susceptibility to Cercospora leaf blight.
Rotation out of soybeans may be helpful.
Foliar and seed treatment fungicides may be useful for managing this disease.
Fungicide classes should be rotated to avoid fungicide resistance development.
Fungicides are registered for use on Cercospora but are not usually needed.
treatments.quote: Crop rotation systems incorporating non-host plants such as corn or small grains can reduce inoculum in crop residue and disrupt the disease cycle. Tillage is also an option to reduce inoculum levels in the field. Soybean varieties vary in their response to C. kikuchii, but none are completely resistant to the pathogen. Fungicides may be used to reduce disease observed in the field, but their use has not been shown to be effective to reduce purple seed stain. ... Fungicide classes should be rotated to avoid fungicide resistance development.

pathogen_scientific_name.quote: Charcoal rot, caused by the soil- and seed-borne fungus Macrophomina phaseolina , is an economically important disease on soybean

type_of_disease.quote: Charcoal rot, caused by the soil- and seed-borne fungus Macrophomina phaseolina

value: Root
Stem
Foliar
Pod
Seed
Vascular
affected_parts.quote: On adult plants, M. phaseolina causes lesions on the root, stem, pod, and seed. Microsclerotia, which are produced in the vascular tissue and the pith

summary.value: Symptoms vary by growth stage: brown to dark spots on cotyledons at seedling stage, reddish-brown circular to oblong lesions on unifoliate leaves turning dark brown to black, and mid- to late-season light brown to grey superficial stem lesions, with microsclerotia in vascular tissue, pith, and pods, and a marbled gray appearance in cross-sections of the lower stem and taproot.
summary.quote: At the seedling stage, typical symptoms include , brown to dark spots on cotyledons. At the unifoliate leaf stage (VC), circular to oblong, reddish-brown, lesions that eventually turn dark brown to black may be observed. Mid to late season aboveground symptoms include light brown to grey superficial lesions in the stem. A characteristic sign of the pathogen on soybean is the presence of microsclerotia in the vascular tissue, in the pith, and mature and dry pods. Cross-sections of the lower stem and taproot may become streaked with gray hyphae and microsclerotia, giving a marbled appearance

diagnostic_features.value: Silvery-grey color in the sub-epidermal tissue of stem and roots caused by microsclerotia formed inside the vascular system is typical and is the source of the common name 'charcoal rot'; plants wilt and die prematurely with leaves remaining attached to petioles; small black specks may form beneath the epidermis giving a charcoal-sprinkled appearance.
diagnostic_features.quote: This silvery-grey color in the sub epidermal tissue of stem and roots is typical and is the source of the common name of the disease "charcoal rot." ... soybean plants will wilt (Figure 5) and die prematurely with leaves remaining attached to petioles. Lower stems and taproots display a darker color than healthy plants, and also exhibit internal grey discoloration due to the presence of microsclerotia when stems are split.

value: Crop rotation to non-hosts (e.g., cereal grains) to reduce survival of microsclerotia in soils
Manage fields to reduce or avoid drought stress; use irrigation when feasible
Reduced tillage / planting in no-tilled soil to keep soils cooler and reduce moisture stress
Reduced seeding rates / lower plant population density to reduce drought stress
Ensure adequate fertilization
Select cultivars with lower susceptibility (no fully resistant cultivars are available)
Fungicides are not typically recommended for managing charcoal rot
treatments.quote: Manage fields to reduce or avoid drought stress. Reduced tillage may reduce charcoal rot, perhaps due to cooler soils and less moisture stress. Rotation with crops that have relatively low susceptibility to charcoal rot, such as cereal grains, may be beneficial. Reduced seeding rates may also reduce drought stress and charcoal rot. Soybean cultivars may exhibit different levels of susceptibility, but none are fully resistant to this disease.

affected_parts.quote: The initial symptoms of downy mildew are small, light green spots (not water-soaked) on upper leaf surfaces. ... Infection of pods and seed can also occur. Seeds may become covered with a whitish coating of fungal hyphae and spores.

summary.value: Irregularly shaped pale green to light yellow spots on upper leaf surfaces that enlarge into pale to bright yellow spots and may coalesce into large irregular brown areas; older lesions turn brown with yellow-green margins, with fuzzy tan to gray tufts of fungal growth on the lower leaf surfaces, especially under wet and humid conditions.
summary.quote: Lesions occur on upper leaf surfaces as irregularly shaped, pale green to light yellow spots that enlarge into pale to bright yellow spots. Older lesions turn brown with yellow-green margins. ... On the leaf underside, fuzzy, gray tufts may be seen growing from lesions, particularly when humidity is high or leaves are wet

diagnostic_features.value: Fuzzy, tan-to-gray tufts of fungal growth on the underside of infected leaves distinguish downy mildew from other foliar soybean diseases; infected pods show no external symptoms but the inside of the pod and seed may be covered with a dried, whitish, crusty fungal mass; infected seed can be smaller, dull white, and have cracks in the seed coat.
diagnostic_features.quote: Downy mildew can be distinguished from other foliar soybean diseases by the tufts of tan-colored fungal growth on the underside of infected leaves.

value: Select resistant soybean varieties (many sources of resistance are available)

value: Foliar

affected_parts.quote: Bacterial blight can occur on all above ground plant parts, but is most evident on leaves in the mid to upper canopy. ... Infection can also occur on stems, petioles, pods, and seeds in infected pods. Infected seedlings may be stunted or killed in severe cases.

summary.value: Infections begin as small, angular, water-soaked spots that turn yellow and then brown as the tissue dies; spots are surrounded by yellowish-green halos and often merge to form large dead patches that fall out, giving leaves a ragged appearance.
summary.quote: Infections begin as small, angular, water-soaked spots that turn yellow and then brown as the tissue dies. The spots darken and are surrounded by yellowish-green halos. Spots often merge to form large, dead patches on the leaves. The dead tissue may fall out giving the leaves a ragged appearance.

diagnostic_features.value: Angular, vein-limited lesions with water-soaked tissue and a characteristic yellowish-green halo; bacterial blight is most common in the mid to upper canopy on younger leaves, with green leaves below the affected area, distinguishing it from brown spot which appears on older, lower leaves.
diagnostic_features.quote: Symptoms in later growth stages include angular lesions, which begin as small yellow to brown spots on the leaves ... A yellowish-green "halo" will appear around the edge of the water-soaked tissue ... However, bacterial blight will be in the mid to upper canopy and have green leaves below the affected area as the crop goes into the flowering stage of development.

value: Septoria brown spot (Septoria glycines)
Soybean rust
Bacterial pustule
look_alikes.quote: Both of these foliar diseases can start in the lower portion of the canopy and may be confused with Septoria leaf spot or soybean rust. ... Symptoms of bacterial pustule are very similar to those of bacterial blight during the early stages of development

value: Plant resistant or less-susceptible soybean varieties
Plant pathogen-free seed
Rotate soybeans with non-host crops
Avoid field cultivation when foliage is wet
Incorporate crop residue with tillage (deep tillage where disease is severe) to reduce inoculum
Copper fungicides labeled for bacterial blight, applied early in the disease cycle
treatments.quote: Resistance. Cultivars that are not highly susceptible to the disease should be considered in fields with a history of bacterial disease problems. Crop rotation. Crop rotation can be an effective method to avoid inoculum from a previously infected crop. Tillage. Incorporating crop residue by tillage will reduce the amount of inoculum available in the spring to infect plants. Cultivation. To prevent the spread of disease, limit cultivation to times when the foliage is dry. Fungicides. Copper fungicides are labeled for con- trol of bacterial blight on soybeans but need to be applied early in the disease cycle to be effective.

pathogen_scientific_name.quote: Bacterial Pustule is caused by the bacteria Xanthomonas axonopodis (syn. campestris ) pv glycines .

type_of_disease.quote: Foliar fungicides are not effective against this bacterial disease.

value: Foliar
Pod
affected_parts.quote: Light-colored, raised blisters (pustules) often develop in the center of lesions on both the upper and lower surface of leaves. ... Small raised spots may also develop on pods.

summary.value: Symptoms begin as small, light green or yellowish spots (without water-soaking), with light-colored raised blisters (pustules) developing in the center of lesions on both upper and lower leaf surfaces; lesions can coalesce into large irregular patches of dead tissue, giving leaves a ragged appearance and possibly causing premature defoliation. Small raised spots may also develop on pods.
summary.quote: Symptoms begin as small, light green spots (without water-soaking). Light-colored, raised blisters (pustules) often develop in the center of lesions on both the upper and lower surface of leaves. The lesions can grow together into large, irregular patches of dead tissue. Loss of tissue in the infected areas may give the leaves a ragged appearance like bacterial blight. Small raised spots may also develop on pods.

diagnostic_features.value: Bacterial pustules will not have a natural opening in the pustule, or masses of spores like those of soybean rust; raised necrotic centers and lack of spores when observed under a microscope distinguish bacterial pustules from soybean rust.
diagnostic_features.quote: Bacterial pustules will differ from those of soybean rust, however, because they will not have a natural opening in the pustule, or masses of spores like those of soybean rust.

value: soybean rust
bacterial blight
brown spot
look_alikes.quote: Because this pathogen produces a pustule, it can easily be confused with soybean rust, a much more serious disease. ... Loss of tissue in the infected areas may give the leaves a ragged appearance like bacterial blight.

value: Use resistant cultivars / replace susceptible varieties with resistant ones
Use disease-free / certified, pathogen-free seed; do not use seed from infected plants
Avoid moving equipment through fields or scouting / cultivating when foliage is wet
Crop rotation out of soybeans until infested debris has decomposed
Tillage to eliminate inoculum in crop residue (can shorten rotation where soil erosion is not an issue)
Copper-based sprays can be used to control bacterial diseases of soybean
Foliar fungicides are not effective against this bacterial disease
In low-pressure regions (e.g., NY) outbreaks rarely reach epidemic proportions and require no management
treatments.quote: There are high levels of resistance to bacterial pustule in most soybean varieties. Should bacterial pustule develop in a field, a note of the variety should be made, and it should be replaced in future years with resistant ones. Since the disease can be seed borne, it is important to use disease-free, certified seed. If a field develops bacteria pustule, it should be rotated out of soybeans until all infested debris has decomposed. Tillage can shorten the length of rotation where soil erosion is not an issue. Avoid moving equipment through fields or scouting when the foliage is wet. Foliar fungicides are not effective against bacterial pustule because it is caused by a bacterial pathogen, not a fungus.

Rotate soybeans with a non-host crop (e.g., alfalfa, corn, small grains)
Tillage / bury infested residue to reduce survival of C. sojina
Foliar fungicides applied during late flowering and early pod set to pod filling stages
Seed treatment fungicides may provide some control
treatments.quote: Resistant soybean varieties are available and should be used where disease is a potential problem... Rotating to a non-host crop and tillage will reduce survival of C. sojina . Crops not susceptible to this pathogen are alfalfa, corn, and small grains. Foliar fungicides applied during late flowering and early pod set to pod filling stages can reduce frogeye leaf spot.

pathogen_scientific_name.quote: Phytophthora root and stem rot of soybean is caused by Phytophthora sojae .

type_of_disease.quote: P. sojae is not a true fungus, but belongs to a group of fungal like organisms called oomycetes (water molds).

value: Seed
Root
Stem
Foliar
Vascular
Whole plant
affected_parts.quote: Seeds, seedlings, and plants in all stages of growth are susceptible to disease... Infections begin in the roots and then spreads upward to the lower nodes of the plant... The roots and lower stem turn a dark brown color, the leaves of the plant turn yellow and necrotic... As P. sojae colonizes the plant, the vascular system and lower stem tissues can occur on the lower stems.

summary.value: Early-season seed rot and pre-/postemergence damping off; on older plants, infected stems appear bruised and soft, secondary roots are rotted, leaves turn yellow and plants wilt and die, with a characteristic dark brown lesion on the lower stem extending up from the taproot.
summary.quote: Early season symptoms include seed rot and pre- and postemergence damping off. The most characteristic symptom of Phytophthora root and stem rot is a dark brown lesion on the lower stem that extends up from the taproot of the plant.

diagnostic_features.value: A dark chocolate-brown discoloration of the stem extends from below the soil line upward, often reaching several nodes and girdling the stem; wilted yellow leaves typically remain attached to the plant after death; plants are often killed in patches or sections of rows.
diagnostic_features.quote: a dark chocolate-brown discoloration of the stem often extends from below the soil line upward into lower parts of the plant. Leaves turn yellow, wilt, and typically stay attached after plant death. Plants are often killed in patches or in sections of rows.

value: Pythium (similar damage on seeds and seedlings)
Stem canker (usually starts at a node and spreads up and down the stem)
look_alikes.quote: It is important to recognize that Pythium is a related pathogen that causes similar damage and symptoms on seeds and seedlings... This disease can be confused with stem canker, which usually starts at a node and spreads up and down the stems of infected plants.

value: Plant resistant cultivars (Rps genes such as Rps1c, Rps1k, Rps1a, Rps3) for race-specific resistance
Plant cultivars with high levels of field tolerance / partial resistance where Rps genes are overcome
Improve soil drainage / reduce saturated soil conditions
Apply seed treatments or soil-applied fungicides targeting oomycetes (mefenoxam / Apron XL or metalaxyl / Apron at highest labeled rates)
Crop rotation and tillage may provide some benefit
treatments.quote: Try to create a field environment that reduces the likelihood of saturated soil by taking steps to increase drainage where possible. Treatment of seed with the highest labeled rates of fungicidal compounds such as mefenoxam (Apron XL®) or metalaxyl (Apron®) can be beneficial. In all areas where Phytophthora root and stem rot has been a problem, resistant cultivars should be planted... Crop rotation and tillage may be of some benefit.

conflicts.note: UMN Extension describes P. sojae as 'a fungal-like pathogen' but does not explicitly classify it; NC State and CPN both classify it as Oomycete.

pathogen_scientific_name.quote: Soybean rust (SBR), caused by the fungus Phakopsora pachyrhizi , is one of the most important diseases of soybean in several regions around the world.

type_of_disease.quote: The causal agent is the basidiomycete fungus, Phakopsora pachyrhizi.

value: Foliar
Stem
Pod
affected_parts.quote: Lesions tend to be angular with 2-5 mm diameter, and can affect not only leaves, but may be observed on petioles, pods, and stems

summary.value: Symptoms begin as very small tan to brown, brick red, gray-green, or reddish-brown angular lesions (2-5 mm) on the lower-canopy leaves; small pustules develop within the lesions on the underside of leaves and break open to release masses of tan urediniospores. Lesions may also occur on petioles, pods, and stems. Heavily infected plants turn yellow, defoliate prematurely, and may mature early.
summary.quote: A common symptom of soybean rust is the formation of gray-green, tan to dark-brown, or reddish-brown lesions that may become erumpant under the lesions on leaflets. Lesions tend to be angular with 2-5 mm diameter, and can affect not only leaves, but may be observed on petioles, pods, and stems ( Figure 1 ). Lesions are associated with leaf chlorosis; high lesion density can cause defoliation and early maturity of the plant

diagnostic_features.value: Small angular lesions (2-5 mm) on the underside of lower-canopy leaves containing pustules that break open to release masses of tan urediniospores; pustules and spore masses are visible with a 20x hand lens. SBR is most often found in the lower canopy at or after flowering stage.
diagnostic_features.quote: Small pustules (blisters) develop in the lesions, which break open and release masses of tan spores. The lesions and pustules, which can be seen with a 20X hand lens, may also appear on pods and stems.

value: Other common leaf diseases of soybean (early symptoms, before pustules develop)
look_alikes.quote: Early symptoms of soybean rust (before pustules develop) are difficult to distinguish from other common leaf diseases on soybean.

value: The primary management of rust is through the use of fungicide applications
Fungicide applications made between R3 and R5 may provide the most yield protection for soybeans
Different modes of action should be used if more than one fungicide is needed in a growing season to manage fungicide resistance
Fungicides are likely not needed if soybean rust is not within 100 miles of a field
Regularly scout fields throughout the growing season to monitor for foliar diseases like rust
Scout the lower canopy of fields weekly, especially during wet weather after soybeans begin to flower
Soybean rust must be diagnosed at an early stage to be successfully managed; cultural practices have had little effect on the disease
Soybean varieties resistant to soybean rust are in development
If SBR is identified prior to the R5 growth stage, fungicide application would be recommended to prevent potential loss in yield
Strobilurin and triazole fungicides are the two classes currently recommended to control SBR
Careful and diligent scouting of both soybean fields and kudzu patches in the southern states
Sentinel plots are scouted weekly for SBR and act as an early warning system for growers
treatments.quote: The primary management of rust is through the use of fungicide applications. Fungicide applications made between R3 and R5 may provide the most yield protection for soybeans. Several fungicides are effective at reducing damages caused by rust, and efficacy can be found on the NC State Soybean Extension portal . Fungicide resistance is a concern with foliar pathogens, and different modes of action should be used if more than one fungicide is needed in a growing season. Fungicides are likely not needed if soybean rust is not within 100 miles of a field.

conflicts.note: NC State uses 'Asian soybean rust' to specify the aggressive Phakopsora pachyrhizi-caused form; UMN notes a second milder species P. meibomiae also causes soybean rust.

conflicts.alternative_value: Phakopsora pachyrhizi and Phakopsora meibomiae (two species cause soybean rust)
conflicts.note: UMN identifies two causal species: P. pachyrhizi (aggressive Asian pathogen) and P. meibomiae (mild pathogen). NC State and Ohio State name only P. pachyrhizi.
Ohio State describes only foliar symptoms; NC State and UMN report lesions on petioles, pods, and stems as well.

pathogen_scientific_name.quote: Sclerotinia stem rot of soybean is caused by the fungus Sclerotinia sclerotiorum .

type_of_disease.quote: Sclerotinia stem rot of soybean is caused by the fungus Sclerotinia sclerotiorum .

value: Foliar
Stem
Pod

affected_parts.quote: Diseased plants first appear wilted and with grayish green leaves that later turn brown to tan (Figure 2). The stems also have grayish to bleached white lesions that are covered by white fluffy mycelia under conditions of high moisture (Figures 3A and 3B). In later stages, sclerotia can be observed inside or outside of the stems and pods (Figures 3C and 3D). If the seeds were infected during the early stages of development, they may have a flat and shriveled appearance, or may be replaced by sclerotia.

summary.value: Diseased plants first appear wilted with grayish green leaves that turn brown to tan; stems develop grayish to bleached white lesions covered by white fluffy mycelia under high moisture, and sclerotia form inside or outside stems and pods.
summary.quote: Diseased plants first appear wilted and with grayish green leaves that later turn brown to tan (Figure 2). The stems also have grayish to bleached white lesions that are covered by white fluffy mycelia under conditions of high moisture (Figures 3A and 3B). In later stages, sclerotia can be observed inside or outside of the stems and pods (Figures 3C and 3D).

diagnostic_features.value: White fluffy mycelia covering grayish to bleached white stem lesions under high moisture, with black, hard, irregular sclerotia (with pink to white center) produced inside or outside stems and pods; infected seeds may be flat and shriveled or replaced by sclerotia.
diagnostic_features.quote: This fungus produces a survival structure called a sclerotium (pl. sclerotia), which is black, hard and irregular in shape with a pink to white center (Figure 1A)... The stems also have grayish to bleached white lesions that are covered by white fluffy mycelia under conditions of high moisture (Figures 3A and 3B). In later stages, sclerotia can be observed inside or outside of the stems and pods (Figures 3C and 3D). If the seeds were infected during the early stages of development, they may have a flat and shriveled appearance, or may be replaced by sclerotia.

value: Bird's nest fungi
look_alikes.quote: Bird's nest fungi can be confused with white mold apothecia.

value: Cultivars with high levels of partial resistance are commercially available; no complete resistance is reported.
Selection of a soybean cultivar should take into account an open canopy of upright leaves to minimize a favorable environment.
Use of long crop rotation with non-host crops such as corn, wheat, or small grains to minimize inoculum build-up.
Avoid rotation with beans, peas, sunflowers, and cole crops (Brassicaceae) in fields with white mold risk.
Good weed management is critical because many broadleaf weeds are hosts for the pathogen.
Some herbicides may suppress the activity of the fungus or disrupt germination of sclerotia.
Tillage is recommended for fields with first occurrence to bury sclerotia; reduced tillage is preferred for fields with a long history of the disease.
Reduction of seeding rate may improve air circulation, reducing humidity and increasing temperature within the canopy.
Increasing row spacing to 30 inch rows may avoid complete canopy closure; low plant populations or wide rows may help reduce severity.
Where irrigation is used, reduce irrigation frequency during flowering.

Foliar fungicides such as Boscalid have provided good efficacy in reducing disease incidence and severity; rotation of active ingredients should be considered. Application timing is critical (immediately before infection); efficacy can be inconsistent.
Lactofen is an herbicide able to modify soybean canopy, delay flowering and induce systemic acquired resistance response.
Biological control with antagonistic fungi (e.g., Coniothyrium minitans) may be applied to soil to colonize and reduce sclerotia numbers.
Avoid introduction of sclerotia in the field; seed should be free of Sclerotinia contamination and infection.
Seed-lots can be cleaned by spiral separators and gravity tables.
Avoid harvesting heavily infested fields until the end of the season as combines can become contaminated with sclerotia.
Seed treatments with fungicides could be used to treat infected seed.
treatments.quote: Host resistance: There is no complete resistance reported for Sclerotinia stem rot of soybean. However, cultivars with high levels of partial resistance are commercially available... Use of long crop rotation... Corn and wheat can be used in crop rotation with soybean to minimize inoculum build-up over time... Tillage is recommended for fields with first occurrence of Sclerotinia stem rot in order to bury the sclerotia... Boscalid has provided good efficacy in reducing disease incidence and severity compared to the untreated control... Lactofen is an herbicide able to modify soybean canopy... Biological control with the fungus Coniothyrium minitans... Avoid introduction of sclerotia in the field... Seed treatments with fungicides could be used to treat infected seed.

conflicts.canonical_value: Sclerotinia Stem Rot (White Mold) of Soybean
conflicts.note: Crop Protection Network uses 'White Mold of Soybean' as the primary name; UMN uses 'Sclerotinia stem rot (white mold) on soybean'. All three refer to the same disease caused by Sclerotinia sclerotiorum.
UMN Extension does not explicitly list foliar tissue as affected; Ohioline and Crop Protection Network describe foliar symptoms (yellowing, wilting, leaves turning brown/tan).

pathogen_scientific_name.quote: Southern blight is caused by Agroathelia rolfsii , a soilborne fungus that has a wide host range of more than 200 plant species.

type_of_disease.quote: Southern blight, caused by the soilborne fungus Sclerotium rolfsii is a serious disease of a wide variety of plants

value: Foliar
Stem
Root
Seed

affected_parts.quote: S. rolfsii usually infects the lower stem near the soil surface. With some plants, the roots may become infected.

summary.value: Disease begins with a small, water-soaked lesion on the lower stem at or near the soil surface that spreads rapidly to girdle the stem, causing wilt; a white mat of mycelium develops at the lesion site followed by mustard-seed-sized sclerotia.
summary.quote: disease begins with a small, water-soaked lesion on the lower stem at or near the soil surface ( Figure 1 ). The lesion spreads rapidly to girdle the stem ( Figure 2 ). On many herbaceous plants and seedlings, the girdling lesion will quickly cause the plant to wilt

diagnostic_features.value: White mat of mycelium at the lesion site that spreads onto nearby soil surface, with small (0.5-1 mm), white, round, fuzzy mycelial bodies (sclerotia) that become smooth and light tan, brown or black; sclerotia are about the size of mustard seeds.
diagnostic_features.quote: a white mat of mycelium develops at the lesion site ( Figure 6 ). This white mat will often spread out onto the nearby soil surface. Shortly after the mycelial mat develops, small (0.5-1 mm), white, round, fuzzy mycelial bodies begin to appear ( Figure 7 ). These mustard-seed-sized structures, called sclerotia, soon become smooth and light tan, brown or black in color

value: Only pathogen-free plants, cuttings or seeds should be purchased from a reputable dealer
Removal of infected plants is an important aspect of disease control
Some localized soil removal and replacement may help to control S. rolfsii
Treating the soil with heat (including solarization), fungicides or fumigants, cultural manipulations, organic amendments, fertilizers, or biological treatments
Treat beds or bulk soil with aerated steam. All areas must be brought to a temperature of 160-180 degrees F for 30 minutes
Solarization for southern blight control has been beneficial in select situations in the southern United States
The soil fungicide pentachloronitrobenzene (PCNB) has been used on peanuts and some other crops since the 1940s
Azoxystrobin applied as preplant and postplant furrow treatments significantly controlled southern blight
The fungicides azoxystrobin, flutolanil, flutolanil + thiophanate-methyl, and/or tebuconazole are labeled for pre- and post plant drenches
Fumigants, such as metam sodium or dazomet (granular), are toxic to sclerotia and mycelium in the soil
A soil insecticide (chlorpyrifos) is also labeled for use as a soil treatment to control S. rolfsii in certain peanut cropping situations
Cultural modifications for management of S. rolfsii in the landscape include deep plowing, lime additions, aerification, and thatch removal
Keeping the soil pH at 6.5 by the addition of lime will help to prevent rapid fungal growth
Addition of organic amendments such as compost, oat or corn straw, or cotton gin trash to soil sometimes reduces southern blight incidence and development
Treatments with ammonium, calcium nitrate or calcium sulfate may help control southern blight
Biological control of S. rolfsii has been achieved to some degree with bacteria (Bacillus subtilis), actinomycetes, a mycorrhizal fungus, or certain Trichoderma species
When infested fields are rotated to corn, inoculum levels in the soil slowly decrease
Peanut rotations with cotton, 'Pensacola' bahia grass (Paspalum notatum), or switch grass (Panicum virgatum) resulted in a decreased incidence of white mold (stem rot) in peanut fields
On peanuts, good disease control has been achieved by foliar applications of azoxystrobin products, flutolanil, or tebuconazole
treatments.quote: Management of Southern Blight ... disease management involves exclusionary practices, plant removal, soil removal or treatment, plant treatment, crop rotation, use of resistant varieties, or a combination of these practices.

conflicts.note: APS uses the older anamorph name Sclerotium rolfsii with teleomorph Athelia rolfsii; Crop Protection Network uses the current accepted name Agroathelia rolfsii. Taxonomy validation data was not provided.
Crop-specific naming variant; APS lists synonyms 'Southern stem blight' and 'White mold' as well.
